Councillor Kirillov.initial_description: Government official sent from Tiflis by Vorontsov to summon Hadji Murad for a meeting with General Argutinski; he also delivers overdue payment
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_102: Fat, bald, civilian official sent from Tiflis to deliver Vorontsov's message and Hadji Murad's allowance; unarmed bureaucrat contemptuous of him

Tavlinian Spy.name: Tavlinian Spy
Tavlinian Spy.initial_description: Fleshy, swarthy hooded spy from the hills bringing news about the failed rescue attempt on HM's family

Old Spy.name: Old Spy
Old Spy.initial_description: Thin old man, second hooded spy from the hills delivering the same bad news

Tavlinian Messenger (fleshy).name: Tavlinian Messenger (fleshy)
Tavlinian Messenger (fleshy).initial_description: Fleshy, swarthy Tavlinian man; one of two messengers bearing bad news from the mountains

Peter Nikolaevich Kamenev.name: Peter Nikolaevich Kamenev
Peter Nikolaevich Kamenev.initial_description: Officer who formerly served with Major Petrov, arrives on horseback with escort bearing news
arc_notes: Chunk chunk_109: Russian officer, formerly served under the major; arrives at night bearing Hadji Murad's severed head in a sack, delivers it matter-of-factly, announces HM 'died like a hero'

Chikhirev.name: Chikhirev
Chikhirev.initial_description: Don Cossack in Kamenev's escort; carries the sack containing Hadji Murad's head

Kamenev.name: Kamenev
Kamenev.initial_description: Russian officer tasked with carrying and displaying Hadji Murad's severed head to forts and aouls as a trophy of war

Nazarov.name: Nazarov
Nazarov.initial_description: Non-commissioned Cossack officer, Cross of St. George recipient; young, brown-haired Old Believer who supports widowed mother and siblings; assigned to escort Hadji Murad

Ferapontov.name: Ferapontov
Ferapontov.initial_description: Tall, thin Cossack; known thief and plunderer; previously sold gunpowder to Gamzalo

Ignatov.name: Ignatov
Ignatov.initial_description: Sturdy, boastful Cossack peasant, near end of service

Petrakov.name: Petrakov
Petrakov.initial_description: Young fair-haired Cossack, his mother's only son; amiable and jolly

Karganov.name: Karganov
Karganov.initial_description: Military commander of the district who leads the militia and Cossack force that surrounds Hadji Murad in the thicket

Hadji Aga.name: Hadji Aga
Hadji Aga.initial_description: Hadji Aga of Mekhtuli — once Hadji Murad's kunak, now Russian-aligned; arrives with ~200 followers and leads the assault

locations.name: Midsummer Fields
locations.description: Lush agricultural landscape with hay-harvested and rye-ripening fields, wildflowers in full bloom, a ditch with a crimson thistle, and a large ploughed black-earth field belonging to a landed proprietor

locations.name: Ploughed Black-Earth Field
locations.description: Large, evenly furrowed, moist black-earth field belonging to a landed proprietor. Lifeless, no grass or plant — except the solitary battered thistle the narrator encounters again.

locations.name: Makhmet Aoul
locations.description: Hostile Chechen village fifteen miles from Russian territory; filled with kizyak smoke, crowded saklyas like honeycomb cells, a fountain below — sounds of cattle, sheep, disputing men, women and children.

locations.name: Chechen Aoul (Arrival Village)
locations.description: A hillside Chechen village with narrow side streets; saklytas built into the hillside with rooftop sleeping areas, wooden-heeled slippers, clay chimneys; intimate domestic space contrasting with Hadji Murad's former martial grandeur.

locations.name: Sado's Saklya
locations.description: Interior of a Chechen mountain dwelling — clay-plastered, whitewashed walls; weapons hung on nails between gleaming basins; cushions along the front wall; earthen floor. Domestic, intimate, martial.

locations.name: Mitchit Aoul
locations.description: Nearby Chechen village mentioned as having had its hay burned by Russian troops — offscreen but dramatically present as evidence of imperial violence.

locations.name: Sado's Saklya (Aoul interior)
locations.description: Interior of Sado's Chechen house; felt mat floor, wooden door with audible steps outside; ritual space of kunak hospitality and conspiratorial planning.

locations.name: Argun River bend
locations.description: Where the river Argun bends by a cliff; a forest glade nearby holds two haystacks where Hadji Murad's four murids wait; remote meeting point for fugitives.

locations.name: Sado's Saklya (Interior)
locations.description: Interior of Sado's Chechen mountain house: low round table, cushioned floor seating; domestic intimacy contrasting with the danger outside; warm, lamp-lit, private

locations.name: Vozvizhensk Fort
locations.description: Russian advanced fort approximately ten miles from Sado's aoul; sends nightly ambush patrols beyond the Shahgirinsk Gate into the surrounding forest.

locations.name: Shahgirinsk Gate
locations.description: Gate of Vozvizhensk Fort beyond which Russian patrols venture into the forested darkness; the boundary between fort-safety and Chechen wilderness.

locations.name: Ambush Plane Tree
locations.description: Blackened trunk of a broken plane tree ~520 paces from the fort gate; the standard Russian night-ambush position in the surrounding forest.

locations.name: Night Ambush Outpost (near Fort)
locations.description: Forest edge outpost, plane trees, night sky; guards positioned to prevent mountaineers from moving a cannon unobserved toward the Russian fort

locations.name: Caucasus Night Encampment (Plane Tree Grove)
locations.description: A forested outpost position where Russian soldiers keep night watch; plane trees, wind, stars, and the howling of jackals define the environment.

locations.name: Vozdvizhensk Fort Interior (Vorontsov's House)
locations.description: Best house in the fort, still lit while barracks are dark; home of Simon Vorontsov, the regimental commander

locations.name: Vorontsov's House (Fort Interior)
locations.description: Spacious drawing room with carpeted floor, rich curtains, four-candle card table; the most luxurious dwelling in the Vozdvizhensk Fort; midnight domestic theatre of imperial privilege

locations.name: Poltoratsky's Quarters
locations.description: A small shared house near the fort; spartan billet for Poltoratsky and Tikhonov

locations.name: Ravine Outside Aoul
locations.description: A forested ravine with rapidly running water at the bottom, crossed by Hadji Murad during his nighttime escape from the aoul; marks the boundary between the village world and the wilderness

locations.name: Forest Glade Camp
locations.description: A hidden clearing in the dark forest where Hadji Murad's murids wait around a bonfire; horses hobbled and saddled, camp quickly extinguished on Hadji Murad's order; transitional refuge between Chechen territory and Russian lines

locations.name: Shagirinsk Gate
locations.description: Russian military checkpoint / fortification gate six miles from the Shalin glade woodcutting site; Poltoratsky's companies march out from here before dawn

locations.name: Forest Outpost Ravine
locations.description: Skirts of a forest above a bare descending ravine slope; Russian outpost position where shots are exchanged with Chechen forces

locations.name: Ravine Outpost / Forest Skirt (near Vozdvizhensk)
locations.description: Forested ravine edge where Russian outposts hold a line; open descending slope visible across the ravine. Scene of Avdeev's wounding. Contiguous with the Shalin Glade operational zone.

locations.name: Vorontsov Drawing Room (Vozdvizhensk)
locations.description: The domestic interior of the fort commander's house; site of the first informal Russian-Hadji Murad social encounter; armchairs, coffee service, child at play

locations.name: Meller-Zakomelsky's Residence (Waiting Room)
locations.description: The general's house in Vozdvizhensk or nearby garrison; the waiting room where Hadji Murad is held signals subordination and bureaucratic power over him.

locations.name: Fort Hospital Ward
locations.description: Small wooden building roofed with boards at the entrance of Vozdvizhensk Fort; common ward with four beds, currently holding four patients before Avdeev's arrival

locations.name: Avdeev Family Farm
locations.description: Rural Russian peasant homestead; frozen threshing floor under moonlight; birch brooms, oat sheaves; hard winter morning

locations.name: Avdeev Family Farm (Threshing Floor)
locations.description: Rural peasant homestead threshing floor, pre-dawn winter setting, frozen snow underfoot; domestic counterpart to the Caucasus front where Avdeev is dying.

locations.name: Avdeev Family Home (village interior)
locations.description: Rural Russian peasant household; well-to-do family with hidden savings; domestic space of grief and domestic conflict over absent conscript

locations.name: Avdeev Family Farmstead (Town-Market Visit)
locations.description: Small Russian peasant village setting; old man drives loaded sledges to town inn where the letter is read aloud.

locations.name: Tiflis Viceregal Palace
locations.description: Michael Vorontsov's Tiflis palace: wide porch, sentinel, drawing room with thirty dinner guests. Seat of Russian imperial power in the Caucasus.

locations.name: Tiflis Viceregal Dining Room
locations.description: Grand dining room in Vorontsov's palace; long table with thirty guests, footmen in livery and knee-breeches, silver tureen — theatre of imperial social ritual.

locations.name: Temir Khan Shura
locations.description: Town raided by Hadji Murad in 1849 in broad daylight; shops plundered; referenced as part of his growing legend at the Tiflis dinner.

locations.name: Vorontsov's Waiting Room (Tiflis Palace)
locations.description: Antechamber of the Tiflis Viceregal Palace where petitioners of all kinds queue: generals, merchants, widows, Georgian nobles, officials, and now Hadji Murad.

locations.name: Tiflis New Theater
locations.description: Newly built theater decorated in Oriental style; site of Italian opera performance; Hadji Murad attends with Loris-Melikov and is observed by the Tiflis social elite

locations.name: Vorontsov Palace Ballroom
locations.description: Large, brightly lit reception hall in the Viceregal Palace; band hidden among trees; site of the Monday evening party where Hadji Murad encounters Russian high society for the first time

locations.name: Tselmess
locations.description: Hadji Murad's birthplace — small aoul 'the size of an ass's head,' two cannon-shots from Khunzakh.

locations.name: Khunzakh
locations.description: Seat of the Avar Khans; HM's family was closely connected through wet-nursing relationships.

locations.name: Khansha's Palace
locations.description: The palace where Hadji Murad grew up alongside the three young Khans; site of privilege, loyalty, and the world he lost. Part of his backstory memory, not a present-day location.

locations.name: Grandfather's Farmstead
locations.description: Remote farmstead of Hadji Murad's maternal grandfather, a silversmith. Evoked in childhood memory; signals clan roots and craft tradition.

locations.name: Tiflis (Russian Command HQ)
locations.description: Site of the Russian Commander-in-Chief Baron Rosen; where Umma Khan is corrupted by Russian officers

locations.name: Hamzad's Camp (near Khunzakh)
locations.description: Hamzad's military encampment approached via a mile-long murid escort with singing, shooting, and caracoling; site of the deceptive reception of the Avar Khans and the subsequent massacre.

locations.name: Khunzakh (Khansha's stronghold)
locations.description: The seat of the Avar Khanate; where Hadji Murad warns the Khansha not to send her son — his warning is ignored.

locations.name: Mansokha Path
locations.description: Narrow mountain path beside a 120-yard abyss; site of Hadji Murad's legendary escape from Russian custody

locations.name: Temir-Khan-Shura
locations.description: Destination to which the Russian soldiers were escorting the captured Hadji Murad

locations.name: Avaria
locations.description: Region Hadji Murad was appointed to govern by General Rosen under Russian authority

locations.name: Mansokha Path (abyss)
locations.description: Narrow mountain path above a ~120-yard-deep ravine; site of Hadji Murad's legendary survival leap — already in location graph as top cinematic anchor.

locations.name: Tabasaran
locations.description: Region where Shamil sent Hadji Murad on a punitive/test mission; site of his last successful raid before the break with Shamil

locations.name: Shoushin District
locations.description: Administrative district under Tarkhanov's command; mentioned as context for his presence

locations.name: St. Petersburg (implied courier destination)
locations.description: Imperial capital receiving Vorontsov's Caucasian report; seat of Chernyshov and Nicholas I.

locations.name: Winter Palace Entrance
locations.description: Grand entrance to the St. Petersburg Winter Palace; cold January morning, sledges, coachmen waiting outside in frozen air

locations.name: Winter Palace Waiting-Room and Staircase
locations.description: Carpeted steps, Court lackeys in gala livery, antechamber outside Nicholas's cabinet — the antechamber of absolute imperial power, rendered as theatre of obsequiousness

locations.name: Nicholas's Cabinet (Winter Palace)
locations.description: Lofty reception room in the upper story of the rebuilt Winter Palace — four large windows, portrait of Alexander I, enormous writing table, arm chair for Nicholas, chairs along walls for officials

locations.name: Masquerade Box
locations.description: A private velvet-furnished box at the masquerade venue, kept reserved for Nicholas's assignations

locations.name: Nicholas's Rendezvous Room
locations.description: The private space where Nicholas habitually conducts his sexual encounters

locations.name: Nicholas's Bedroom
locations.description: Spartan room with a hard narrow bed; Nicholas sleeps under a cloak he likens to Napoleon's hat — performative austerity

locations.name: Palace Embankment (St. Petersburg)
locations.description: The waterfront embankment outside the Winter Palace where Nicholas walks each morning in military cloak and cap; site of impromptu encounters with subjects.

locations.name: Winter Palace Small Portico
locations.description: The private exit/entrance Nicholas uses for his morning walk; through it he re-enters from the embankment and observes arriving carriages at the Saltykov entrance.

locations.name: Saltykov Entrance (Winter Palace)
locations.description: A specific entrance to the Winter Palace where Helena Pavlovna's carriage is seen arriving; exterior, briefly glimpsed

locations.name: Nicholas's Cabinet (Report Room)
locations.description: The private cabinet within the imperial apartments where Nicholas receives morning reports from ministers; intimate seat of autocratic power

locations.name: Pompeian Hall (Winter Palace)
locations.description: Imperial dining hall in the Winter Palace where the New Year dinner is hosted; lavish, classicizing décor underscores the theatrical self-presentation of the Romanov court.

locations.name: Pompeian Hall
locations.description: Imperial dining hall where Nicholas hosts state dinner with foreign dignitaries

locations.name: Chechnya March Route
locations.description: Winter Caucasus landscape — stream crossing, tilled fields, forest-clad hills, snowy peaks; the column's path into hostile territory for the January 1852 raid.

locations.name: Unnamed Chechen Aoul (Burned)
locations.description: A Chechen village entered at a run after Cossack pursuit; found deserted; corn, hay, and saklyas ordered burned by Russian soldiers

locations.name: Maize Field Ravine
locations.description: Stubbly maize field crossing off the main road leading toward the forest where the skirmish unfolds; second ravine reveals the aoul

locations.name: Deserted Chechen Aoul (raid site)
locations.description: An unnamed mountain-side aoul evacuated before the Russian column arrived; burned during the raid. Fusable with other destroyed aoul locations (Sado's Aoul remains the named anchor).

locations.name: Vozdvizhensk Fort (return approach)
locations.description: March route back to the fort after patrol; night air, snow hills visible ~100 miles away

locations.name: Sado's Aoul (destroyed)
locations.description: The same Chechen village where Hadji Murad spent his first night before surrendering — now burnt ruins, desecrated mosque and fountain, dead son brought to mosque on horseback

locations.name: Vedeno
locations.description: Shamil's stronghold aoul where Hadji Murad's family is imprisoned; large settlement with public celebrations on Shamil's return

locations.name: Ibrahim Raschid's Saklya
locations.description: House in Vedeno where Hadji Murad's women and children are kept under guard

locations.name: Vedeno Pit-Prison
locations.description: A pit more than seven feet deep used as a prison; Yusuf held here with seven other criminals

locations.name: Shamil's Aoul (Vedeno approach)
locations.description: A large aoul whose entire population lines the streets and rooftops for the Imam's arrival; firing rifles and pistols in celebration.

locations.name: HM Family Saklya (penthouse)
locations.description: Interior of Hadji Murad's family dwelling where his wives watch from the penthouse; Patimat remains inside by the dying hearth.

locations.name: Prison Pit
locations.description: A dark, fetid underground pit where Yusuf and other prisoners are held — they can hear but not see Shamil's triumphal procession.

locations.name: Shamil's Outer Court (Vedeno)
locations.description: Large courtyard at the gates of Shamil's compound, crowded with petitioners, supplicants, and those summoned for judgment; guarded by two armed Lesghians

locations.name: Shamil's Inner Court (Vedeno)
locations.description: Private inner court separated from the outer by gates; penthouse apartment to the left of the gate; seraglio behind a fence dividing men's and women's quarters

locations.name: Shamil's Guest Chamber (Vedeno)
locations.description: Council room in Vedeno where Shamil's six elder advisors assemble; distinct from his private quarters; formal judicial-political space

locations.name: Shamil's Council Chamber (Vedeno)
locations.description: Interior court setting where Shamil sits on a larger cushion, hears criminal cases under Shariat law, and deliberates political strategy with his naib councilors.

locations.name: Shamil's Wives' Quarters (Vedeno)
locations.description: The domestic enclosure behind the fence at Vedeno where Shamil's wives live in separate rooms; site of private marital politics invisible to his public authority

locations.name: Petrovsk Fort (Butler's quarters / Hadji Murad's room)
locations.description: The Russian frontier fort where Hadji Murad is billeted; Butler's room and HM's adjacent room serve as meeting space for their cross-cultural friendship

locations.name: Russian Field Camp (left flank)
locations.description: Military encampment with splendid cloth tents; Vorontsov's command tent; long table set for formal dinner; mountaineers occasionally shell it

locations.name: Nukha
locations.description: Small Muslim town in Transcaucasia; Hadji Murad requests relocation here for proximity to Shamil negotiations and Islamic prayer. Becomes his gilded cage.

locations.name: Nukha House (Hadji Murad's quarters)
locations.description: Five-room house near the mosque and Khan's palace in Nukha; shared with officers, interpreter, and henchmen; serves as Hadji Murad's gilded-cage residence

locations.name: Vozdvizhensk Fort (officers' mess / barracks)
locations.description: Interior of the fort during a military dinner; soldiers quartered in barracks, officers sharing quarters with the Kabarda regiment newcomers.

locations.name: Road beyond Nukha fort
locations.description: Rural road past the fort perimeter; buffaloes, wagons, women with baskets; open country with river visible to the left; site of HM's final ride

locations.name: Benerdzhik Aoul
locations.description: Aoul visible to the right of the road with saklyas and minarets, near the point where HM turns left toward the river.

locations.name: Al-Azani River approach
locations.description: Fields and river to the left of the road; HM's chosen evasion route away from the expected mountain direction.

locations.name: Alazan River Flooded Rice-Fields
locations.description: Boggy, flooded rice paddies between HM's escape route and the Alazan River; impassable in spring flood

locations.name: Shrub Thicket / Night Shelter
locations.description: Patch of higher ground overgrown with shrubs where HM and murids rest horses and pray before pursuers arrive

locations.name: Thicket Ditch (Final Entrenchment)
locations.description: A rice-field bog thicket with an old ditch; Hadji Murad's final defensive position, fortified with cut branches and earth

lines.line: Selaam aleikum, old father. May thy sons live.
Ne habar?
lines.subtext: He wants to know what the old man will hide.

lines.line: The night is cold. A horse and one murid — no more.
My murid. Eldar is his name. He eats what thou givest.
Thy chimney is fresh. A good house stands quiet tonight.
lines.subtext: He has noticed who else may be watching.

lines.line: I do not ride with a banner now. The road chooses the man.
lines.subtext: Admits fall from naib without self-pity.

lines.line: Shamil eats where he eats. I do not speak of him under thy roof.
lines.subtext: Signals break, refuses to curse host's allegiance.

lines.line: Tell me only this — is the path below the wood still open?
lines.subtext: Road stands in for family, escape, life.

lines.line: If Allah wills, we ride before the moon stands over the saklyas.
My son Yusuf is in Shamil's hand. A father hears that in his sleep.
I ask no bread I cannot repay. A saddle, water, silence.
Inshallah, the dogs will sleep tonight. Wake us before the muezzin.
lines.subtext: Testing whether old man will wake only them.

chunk_003_tension_0_55.notes: Lines 4-14 words; Ne habar probes; Shamil not cursed in host's house.

lines.line: Selaam aleikum, old father. The night rode ahead of us.
May thy sons live. Thy roof still stands well.
lines.subtext: Testing whether Shamil's men have been.

lines.line: My murid. Eldar is his name. He sleeps where I sleep.
The horse can stand here tonight. No further.
Ne habar?
lines.subtext: He has read the boy's startled eyes.

lines.line: Sado prays late. Call him, but quietly — no lamp at the door.
May thy coming bring happiness also to this house, sister.
The rifle beside thy master's. The sword also. It is fitting.
lines.subtext: Placing his fate inside household's honor.

lines.line: I will sit. Eldar stands. So it must be tonight.
lines.subtext: Refusing full hospitality warns pursuers close.

lines.line: Of Shamil — not in this house. Tell me only of the road from the north.
lines.subtext: Wants to hear which pass is open.

lines.line: Inshallah, before the second prayer we are gone.

chunk_004_tension_0_55.notes: Composure holds; every courtesy a probe.

lines.line: Selaam aleikum. Thy roof still stands. That is much, in such a season.
lines.subtext: Reading old man's face for what he won't say.

lines.line: Ne habar?
Thy son knows. Good. A son who knows is a roof of his own.
Hares and eagles. Yes. The Mitchit hay will grow again, old father.
lines.subtext: Will not echo curse in another man's saklya.

lines.line: My murid. Eldar is his name.
He will eat from thy hand as I do. His dagger sleeps where mine sleeps.
The horse outside — is there water behind the saklya, or must we go to the stream?
A short night is enough. Before the moon climbs over the ridge, we are gone.
lines.subtext: Warning host not to plan long stay.

lines.line: Tell me only this, old father: is the road to the upper aoul still open to men on foot?
May thy sons live. And the woman at the hearth — may her hands never tire.
lines.subtext: Politeness scaled up because trust scaled down.

lines.line: Eldar. The saddles stay close. The rifles on that nail — we know them by touch.
Inshallah, by the second prayer we are in the wood.

chunk_005_tension_0_55.notes: Fugitive composure; courtesy intensifies with suspicion.

fusion_candidates.reason: Unnamed murid in flight scene likely collapses into Eldar, HM's primary murid companion across the novel; CF-01 applies — <5 scenes, same ally function.
Old man may be a prologue figure before Sado's formal introduction; if distinct, both serve identical kunak-host function and can be fused into Sado for economy.

Both are Sado's household elder figures with kunak-hospitality function; if Sado is portrayed as older, father's warning proverb can be delivered by Sado himself — one household voice instead of two. CF-01 applies: father has <2 scenes, shares hospitality/warning function with Sado.

Sado's Son's only function is to fetch Bata; in a film the summons can be elided (Bata simply enters). Son has zero independent dramatic weight. Absorbing the fetch into Bata's arrival costs nothing. → CF-01, CF-09

Both serve purely as conduits for Hadji Murad's envoy operation; Bata guides, Khan Mahoma speaks — in film one bilingual loyal murid could perform both functions, eliminating the relay structure. However, Khan Mahoma's status as named murid-in-waiting (with a distinct location) and Bata's role as independent Chechen guide give them slightly different thematic colours (insider vs. hired outsider). Propose but do not auto-fuse. CF-01, CF-09 apply; CF-10 gate required.

Both are unnamed domestic figures in a single hospitality scene with identical function (serving, departing); can be compressed to one female figure — the wife — without loss of dramatic content. The daughter's 'sloe-black eyes' detail can be transferred to the wife. [CF-01, CF-02]

Graph pre-flag [0.85]: both are Avdeev's patrol/deathbed companions with near-identical functions; Panov introduced here as patrol NCO — if Serogin also appears only in adjacent patrol/deathbed scenes, merge is economical (CF-01, CF-09).
Both serve as Avdeev's comrades at deathbed with near-identical narrative function; pre-graph confidence 0.85 — confirmed fusable → CF-01

Both are Avdeev's comrades at the night watch with nearly interchangeable functions — pragmatic foil to his emotionalism. Merging into one named soldier preserves the dialogue dynamic while halving the roster. Confirmed by graph (conf 0.85). → CF-01, CF-02

Both are background soldiers on the same watch with identical dramatic function (present, reactive, no independent voice); Bondarenko's half-started dream line is the only distinguishing beat and can be absorbed into a single composite soldier — CF-01 confirmed, confidence 0.90
Both are unnamed-crowd Russian soldiers in Panov's picket; Bondarenko escorts, Nikitin grumbles — single scene, interchangeable function; graph confidence 0.90 confirmed by this chunk

Both are unnamed functionaries in Vorontsov's social circle with single incidental appearances; adjutant's irritation-function and tutor's gloomy-observer-function can merge into one character who registers the moral absurdity of the scene — CF-01, CF-02
Adjutant has zero independent dramatic function here; all social-reaction beats can be absorbed into Poltoratsky as the main Russian POV figure — CF-01, CF-09

Ivan Petrovich is pure off-screen envy-object; his function (the 'free man' Vovilo measures himself against) can be internalised into Vovilo's silent behaviour or a single overheard remark — no independent scene. CF-01 applies.
Tikhonov is a one-line time-keeper with zero independent function; his single remark can be absorbed into ambient dialogue or cut — CF-01 applies

Pre-identified pair (0.85 confidence): Vovilo's serf resentment and Ivan Petrovich as off-screen reference are parallel victim-of-circumstance figures; confirmed by this chunk's Vovilo scene — CF-01, CF-02

Sado's Father served solely as household-welcome figure in prior chunk; Sado now carries the kunak loyalty function completely — Father's single gesture can be absorbed into Sado's farewell action here → CF-01

Both are referenced only as co-departing envoys on the same mission to Vorontsov; neither has independent dramatic weight in this chunk or the wider novel. Pre-graph confidence 0.90 for this pair. CF-01 and CF-09 support fusion into a single envoy figure.

Both are murids in Hadji Murad's inner circle; Khanefi manages household affairs, Gamzalo is the martial guardian. Their functions are adjacent but NOT fully overlapping — Khanefi has a domestic/logistical role, Gamzalo a security/warrior role. CF-03 check: drives are compatible (both loyal to HM) but thematic functions differ enough to KEEP DISTINCT for now. Flag for Phase 5 review; do not fuse yet.

Both function as messenger-guides on the same single mission to the Russian prince; Bata has no independent action or line — can be absorbed into Khan Mahoma's role. Confirmed by co-appearance in this chunk with no distinction in dramatic function.
Both are named officers present only as idle bystanders at the Shalin glade camp with no independent dramatic function; either can absorb the other's minimal presence → CF-01, CF-09
Freze functions identically to Butler here: young officer comrade sent on a task at Poltoratsky's order; < 3 scenes, overlapping role as Poltoratsky's subordinate aide

Freze has exactly one action (rises, moves toward his company) with zero dialogue; his command function is entirely absorbed by Poltoratsky who dominates the scene. Single-scene officer whose sole act — reacting to gunfire — can be cut or folded into Poltoratsky's arrival beat. CF-01 confirmed: <5 scenes, same functional role (Russian officer in field).
Both are Avdeev's comrades at the deathbed with nearly interchangeable functions; this chunk confirms Panov has the only active role — Serogin can be folded in (CF-01, confidence 0.85)

Both are murids with no independent dramatic arc — Khanefi is logistics, Gamzalo is muscle; single composite murid could serve both functions in a compressed film. However Gamzalo grows in dramatic weight later (loyalty vs. violence), so confirm before fusing. → CF-01 candidate, CF-10 gate required.
Both are mid-level Russian generals in chain-of-custody over Hadji Murad; Meller-Zakomelsky appears here only as an off-screen jurisdictional threat — CF-01, CF-09

Kozlovsky appears only as named recipient of Vorontsov's letter, identical chain-of-custody function to Meller-Zakomelsky; pre-graph confidence 0.80 confirmed — both are mid-tier Russian generals with no independent dramatic action

Marya Vasilevna functions as social-lubricant witness; her French commentary to Vorontsov could be absorbed into Vorontsov's own interiority or cut — but she is a named historical figure (CF-04 consideration). Flag for Human-in-the-Loop. → CF-04, CF-10

The aide exists only to relay the general's order; the command can arrive via a brief scene with Meller himself, eliminating the relay function entirely. CF-01 applies: <5 scenes, identical messenger function.
She exists solely as a pretext for Marya Vasilevna's visit; no independent function — CF-01, CF-09

All four are anonymous background patients serving identical function: establish the ward as a place of accumulated suffering and gather around Avdeev. One composite 'Ward Soldier' suffices; CF-01 applies (each has <5 scenes, all share the 'suffering Russian soldier' function). CF-09 confirms fusion economical.

Both appear exclusively as Avdeev's comrades at the deathbed with interchangeable functions (sit with him, write the letter, bear witness). Pre-graph confidence 0.85. This chunk confirms functional overlap: Panov asks the questions, Serogin would write — one composite comrade can hold both beats.
All three are thematic placeholders for 'home' — can be compressed to two figures (father + one woman) without losing the domestic counterpoint image; CF-01 applies
Pre-identified high-confidence (0.85) fusion; Panov confirmed here at deathbed with Avdeev, Serogin's function identical — CF-01, CF-02
Both exist solely to mourn Peter/Avdeev and scold Akim; single parental voice would consolidate grief function. CF-01, CF-02.
Both are unnamed family witnesses with no independent arc; Akim as sole sibling-foil is sufficient. CF-01.
Both function solely as domestic foils revealing the mother's grief; daughter-in-law has one mediating line only — her beat can be absorbed into Akim's reaction

Both are unnamed peasant family members whose only function is to carry the letter to town; either one suffices for the errand. Grandson can be cut, old man does the scene alone. CF-01: <5 scenes each, identical logistical function.

Ivan Petrovich is a named off-screen comparison point for Vovilo; Shopman is similarly peripheral. Both are background males defining someone else's arc. Neither appears; both can be dissolved into context — CF-01, SS-03
Infant with zero agency; exists only to intensify Aksinya's lamentation. Retainable as a prop/detail rather than a character — no fusion needed, simply not a dramatis personae entry

Both appear only as names in this single anecdote; Zolotukhin is merely 'killed' and Pahlen is just the commander whose detachment was attacked — identical narrative function as backstory props for Hadji Murad's legend. One name suffices. [CF-01, CF-09]

The general's sole function is to deliver Hadji Murad's legend and embarrass Vorontsov; Zolotukhin's death is the vehicle. If the general is retained, Zolotukhin can be absorbed (the general describes his own near-death instead). [CF-01]

Both serve as social moderators at the Tiflis dinner; Manana's single intervention (interrupting the general) can be absorbed into Marya Vasilevna's already-established role as social lubricant and host's wife. CF-01: <5 scenes, identical functional role. CF-02: same thematic function (managing imperial social performance).

Both are anonymous table guests whose sole function is supplying Hadji Murad anecdotes that flatter Vorontsov. CF-01: each <2 scenes, purely functional. Can be merged into a single unnamed 'courtier' or absorbed into the Georgian Prince composite.

Single-appearance reaction figure (horror at the widow story). CF-01 candidate: function (aristocratic European reaction shot) can be given to Marya Vasilevna or cut entirely — her 'Quelle horreur' adds color but zero plot weight.
Both are courtier-flatterers at Vorontsov's table with zero independent dramatic weight; chunk confirms single-function overlap → CF-01, CF-02

All six are waiting-room extras with identical dramatic function: to establish imperial petitioner culture. None has individual dramatic weight. For film, reduce to 2-3 representative types — e.g., the widow (human cost), one uniformed officer (military apparatus), one civilian supplicant (civilian machinery). CF-01, CF-09.
Aide-de-Camp can absorb the general's farewell beat (one character shows the bureaucratic ritual of departure). CF-01.
Both are waiting-room petitioners with zero dramatic function; one suffices as background texture — CF-01, CF-09

Both are Caucasian native figures referenced in relation to HM's past; Akhmet Khan is dead and functions only as backstory villain; can be absorbed into a composite antagonist-from-the-past or cut — CF-01 applies
Pre-identified at 0.85 confidence; Akhmet Khan named here as dead backstory antagonist; no independent dramatic weight; consistent with graph-identified fusion candidate — CF-01

KEEP DISTINCT — historical anchor (CF-04); appears here as aide-de-camp, later as life-story recorder; two distinct dramaturgical functions preclude fusion (CF-09 economical test fails — keeping him adds scenes, not reduces)

Both serve as social-lubricant functions within the Vorontsov household scenes; however Marya Vasilevna carries the 'Baryatinsky affair' subplot (cuttable per graph) so she is a CUT candidate, not fusion target — her scenes can be absorbed into background atmosphere (CF-01 partial apply: social-greeter line transferred to unnamed hostess)
Pre-identified CF-0.90: both named only in Vorontsov letters as identical administrative recipients — not present in this chunk but confirmed absent; PROPOSE per CF-10

All three Khan sons appear only as wet-nursing recipients in backstory; dramatically, one single 'Khan son HM's mother nursed' suffices to establish the family bond and the refusal conflict. Consolidate to one unnamed or one-named Khan son (CF-01, CF-09). Rule: CF-01.
Father appears only in this backstory beat as agent of violence; no independent arc. Retain as unnamed violent patriarch — no separate character entry needed. CF-01 applies.
Both are deep-backstory Imam references used only to contextualise Shamil's rise; confirmed by pre-graph [0.85]. If either is retained as a name-drop, the other is redundant. CF-01, CF-02.

Both are named Khans in Hadji Murad's palace youth with near-identical narrative function (childhood bond, lost world). Abu Nutsal is Osman's foster-brother, Umma is HM's sworn brother — the sworn-brother relation is more dramatically potent, so Abu Nutsal can be absorbed. CF-01, CF-09.
Both are backstory Imam references used only to contextualize Shamil's rise; either can be cut, retaining one name — confirmed by graph pre-identification [0.85]

refocalize.scene: Thistle-plucking prologue
refocalize.recommended_pov: TRANSLATE_TO_VISUAL — open on extreme close-up of crimson thistle in ditch, hands (unseen owner) struggling to pluck it, flower destroyed; cut to title. No narrator. Thistle image recurs as motif when Hadji Murad is cornered. Implements NF-04 (cut authorial voice) + NF-08 (single frame use) + SC-06 (translate literary beauty to visual).

refocalize.scene: Author-Narrator's walk across ploughed field and thistle observation
refocalize.recommended_pov: DISSOLVE frame narrator entirely; open on VISUAL of mangled thistle in black field — camera as narrator — then CUT TO Hadji Murad riding into Makhmet at dusk

refocalize.scene: Authorial framing of Hadji Murad's past reputation ('famous for his exploits, who used never to ride out without his banner')
refocalize.recommended_pov: Translate into visual contrast — brief flash of HM in full regalia with banner and murids, then hard cut to cloaked fugitive on narrow street. No voice-over.

refocalize.scene: Old man's eagle/hare proverb and Mitchit raid report
refocalize.current_pov: Third-person omniscient narrator (Tolstoi) — old man's perspective and knowledge
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage through Hadji Murad's reception of the information — his stillness, his reading of the old man's evasion. Camera stays on HM's face, not the old man's. CF-08 / NF-04.

refocalize.scene: Sado's arrival and prayer ritual
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator describing Sado's physical appearance and ritual in detail
refocalize.recommended_pov: HM's POV — we see what he scrutinizes: Sado's face, the door, the sounds outside. HM's watchful tension (listening for danger) drives the staging rather than narrator description. → CF-08, NF-04

refocalize.scene: Sado's daughter enters and is described at length by the narrator
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator (external physical description of the girl)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's arrested attention — his sudden silence when the women enter can be filmed as his POV registering her, making the gendered code visceral and personal rather than descriptive. NF-04 (cut omniscient narrator voice, translate to protagonist behavior/camera).

refocalize.scene: Women serving and departing the saklya
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator observing women's movements and Eldar's statue-like stillness
refocalize.recommended_pov: Hadji Murad's restrained gaze — he does not look at them either; camera stays on his face and hands, registering the cultural code without explanation. [CF-08, NF-04]

refocalize.scene: Chapter II opening — Russian patrol to the ambush plane tree
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator following Panov and the three Russian soldiers
refocalize.recommended_pov: Retain as counterpoint POV (parallel cut with Sado scene) rather than eliminating — the spatial irony of the two worlds sharing one dark night is load-bearing for the anti-imperial theme. Frame via intercut rather than full POV transfer. If a single-POV policy is enforced for the film, restage as Hadji Murad hearing distant Russian boot-sounds / firelight from the fort visible through a shutter crack (CF-08, NF-09).

refocalize.scene: Night ambush outpost — soldiers' idle gossip and smoking
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator following Panov and Avdeev
refocalize.recommended_pov: Retain as-is — Avdeev's POV anchors the common-soldier counterweight to HM's story; restage as Avdeev-centric scene to preserve his voice-profile introduction

refocalize.scene: Russian soldiers' night-watch conversation at the plane tree grove
refocalize.current_pov: Omniscient narrator / Russian soldiers (Panov, Avdeev, Nikitin)
refocalize.recommended_pov: Retain as contrast scene — but frame through HM's awareness if possible (e.g., HM hears Russian voices/jackals from a distance), or keep as inter-cut to establish the human cost on the Russian side without losing HM's line. Per CF-08: refocalize non-protagonist POVs as events HM perceives or later learns of. SC-01 test: scene passes on theme (imperial toll) and filmability, so KEEP with light reframe.

refocalize.scene: Night ambush outpost — soldiers' gossip and Avdeev's homesickness monologue
refocalize.current_pov: Russian soldiers (Avdeev, Panov) — Hadji Murad entirely absent
refocalize.recommended_pov: Retain as HM-adjacent scene; reframe in film as the last ordinary moment before HM's emissary arrives — the soldiers become witnesses to history rather than protagonists; keep short, end on Bata's appearance to anchor viewer in HM's defection arc (CF-08)

refocalize.scene: Scouts' delivery to Vorontsov's house and the colonel's still-burning light
refocalize.recommended_pov: Restage from Bata's or Hadji Murad's perspective (as a scene HM later learns of via Bata's account) — keeps the defection initiative rooted in HM's agency
